content.assessment: Quota and outreach expansion operates within the existing network footprint without requiring new schools or transport reconfiguration, but outreach to disadvantaged applicants in the 1,200+ settlements without educational institutions, and in rural areas with acute teacher shortages, faces access and capacity constraints not addressed by the mechanism itself.
opportunities: Surplus dormitory capacity could support disadvantaged quota-admitted students needing to travel from underserved settlements to concentrated track locations
strengths: Does not require new school construction or capacity expansion, working within the existing 880-school access design
Kindergarten-stage outreach could be delivered through the existing feeder network before the age-10 selection point
threats: Geographic concentration of tracks in Budapest and regional centers means quota-admitted rural students face greater transport burden than urban peers, potentially undermining equitable access even with quotas
Contracting feeder network reduces the pool from which outreach can draw disadvantaged applicants over time
weaknesses: Outreach and exam-preparation support cannot easily reach disadvantaged students in the 1,200+ settlements lacking educational institutions or reliant on a contracting feeder network
Rural schools facing severe teacher shortages and higher rates of unqualified teachers may be poorly positioned to deliver effective outreach or preparation support
